text: Earlier this month, Alibaba released Quen 3.5 with a 400 billion parameter model and a max thinking one that claims to have better benchmarks than Opus 4.5 with beefy requirements to run locally. But just this week, they released the medium series Quen 3.5 models that are almost as powerful as their Max ones and have the ability to run locally on a modern MacBook Pro, claiming to also have better benchmarks than Sonic 4.5, which I don't believe. So, hit subscribe and let's put these two models to the test. Most developers will admit that Sonic 4.5 is a great model, working well with Claude Codes, Co-work, and the whole anthropic suite, making the experience feel premium. But, you have to be online for these models to work. And they're not that cheap. Quen 3.5's Medium series aims to change all of that by making it possible to run a model as good as Sonet 4.5 locally, and people on Twitter are going crazy. But I'm not convinced it's actually as good as Sonic 4.5. So, I'm going to test both these models on a easy, medium, and a hard task and see which one performs better. But before we get into the testing, I have a small confession to make. I'm not actually going to run Quen 3.5 locally because my measly M1 MacBook Pro doesn't have the unified memory to run inference properly. So, I'm going to be using Quen 3.5 35B on open router connected to open code. And I'm going to be running Sonet 4.5 in clawed code on clean mode. So, it's not using any of my skills plugins or MCP tools. We'll start simple and ask the models to build a to-do list from scratch using React and Vit. If you look at quen 3.5, they both have a similar styling and haven't overwritten the default styling that comes with vit. But again, I can add a to item. And here we have a few other options. So we can choose the category that it goes into. We can choose the I think severity and maybe a to-do date or a date that it's due. So for test number two, Claude also wins. For the final test, I got these models to modify an existing code base to take a screenshot of a tweet when the user posts the URL inside the app. We'll start off with Claude, which produced the screen page over here, give me the option to change the background and padding. Now, the first time I ran this, I did get an error, which I asked Claude to fix. I'm going to copy the URL for this tweet by Jason, paste it in here, and click capture. And after a few seconds, we get the image down here with the option to download it. And here is the result from Quen with a screen page over here. Again, I'm going to copy the tweet, paste it here. It says extract video instead of extract screenshot. And it starts to capture it, which looks promising. But after a while, we hit a 60-second timeout, which is similar to the error we experienced with Sonnet, but I did ask when to fix it, and it did extend the timeout, but it didn't fix the issue that caused it in the first place. So, it looks like Sonic 4.5 wins all three tests. So, even though on paper Quinn 3.535B should outperform Sonic 4.5, in real world testing, that doesn't seem to be the case. And don't get me wrong, it's really impressive that you can run a 35 billion or even 27 billion parameter model locally on a modern MacBook. But regardless of what people on Twitter are saying about it, there's no way it can outproduce Sonet 4.5 on coding tasks, as you can see from the tests I ran earlier. So why do the benchmarks make it look so good? Well, there is a huge chance that Quen 3.5 was post-trained on specific benchmark questions like SWBench verified so that it performs well on those questions. But a model like Sonet 4.5 would have been post-trained on a much broader and robust data set making it handle more nuance tasks. But not to mention the quen model I tested had 35 billion parameters but only use three billion during inference. Whereas even though Anthropic don't publish their numbers, looking at estimations, Sonet 3 could have been trained on 70 billion parameters and there's no doubt Sonet 4.5 would have much more. So, it's not really fair to compare these models on benchmarks alone. It's always important to do your own research and run your own evals. I mean, there is a reason why
